Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered because I'm working while doing this degree. I'm 29, so I've got bills to pay and can't just be a full-time student. I'd usually carve out chunks of time on weekends and maybe a couple evenings during the week, totaling around 26 hours. Being a hands-on learner, I'd try to do practical exercises rather than just reading, but my schedule meant I couldn't always attend live sessions.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what was happening at work. Some weeks I'd be consistent, other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. Deadlines were the main driver honestly - when something was coming up, I'd find the time somehow, even if it meant sacrificing sleep or social stuff.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing if grades had come back. Sometimes I'd log in because a peer from my study group messaged me about something. I wasn't one of those people constantly checking - I'd go in with a purpose, get what I needed, and get out.
Q4: Not really a routine, no. The LMS never felt like something I naturally gravitated toward. I'd use it when I had to, but it didn't become habitual. My peer support group was more of a routine - we'd meet up or video call regularly, and that kept me on track more than the platform itself.
Q5: The downloadable materials were gold for me because I could take them offline and work through them at my own pace. I'm kinesthetic, so anything I could print out and annotate or work through with my hands was helpful. The video content was decent too - I watched maybe 40 minutes worth on average, but I'd often pause and try things out myself rather than just passively watching.
Q6: The discussion forums I barely touched - only posted three times the whole course. They felt awkward and performative to me. I'd rather talk things through with my actual peers than type out responses for strangers. Some of the reading materials I skimmed or skipped entirely when I felt I could learn the same thing faster through practice.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams, it felt worthwhile. Anything that seemed like busywork or filler, I'd deprioritize. With limited resources and time, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. If my study group said something was important, I'd trust that too.
Q8: Optional stuff almost always got pushed aside unless someone specifically recommended it. Required activities had deadlines and consequences, so they got done. Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford given everything else on my plate. It's not that I didn't want to learn more - I just had to triage.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, print worksheets when I could afford to, and work through problems by hand. I used YouTube tutorials and free resources online because the course materials alone weren't always enough. My peer group shared notes and explained concepts to each other constantly.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with the material but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper, or my study group would meet at someone's place and we'd work through concepts together. None of that shows up in any log, but that's where a lot of my actual learning happened.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, then work with it for days or weeks offline. My internet isn't always reliable, and honestly it's easier to focus without being connected. So one download might represent hours of subsequent study that never got tracked.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches. I remember feeling scared about whether I'd actually pass, especially when work got intense and I fell behind on attendance. My attendance was really low - like 13% - because of work conflicts. Those moments when everything piled up made it hard to even want to log in.
Q13: Yeah, competing responsibilities were constant. Work would demand overtime, or I'd be too mentally exhausted after a shift to engage meaningfully. Fatigue was real - sometimes I'd log in but my brain just wasn't processing anything. Having low resources meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but nothing was actually sinking in. I was going through the motions because I felt like I should be doing something, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions were more about guilt than genuine engagement.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt low-impact compared to assignments. I also avoided some of the heavier reading when I was already overwhelmed. Even knowing something was important, if I was scared about a bigger deadline, the smaller stuff got pushed. My resilience helped me bounce back, but avoidance happened.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated everything. I'd ramp up my LMS usage significantly when something was due. Without a deadline, I'd drift. The structure helped in a way - it forced prioritization. But it also meant my engagement looked really uneven, with spikes around due dates.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially if it was encouraging, I'd feel more motivated to engage with the next thing. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes made me retreat a bit, question whether I was cut out for this. My peers were actually better at giving me useful feedback than the formal course feedback sometimes.
Q18: The course having 16 online components was a lot to manage. The structure was okay but not great for someone like me who learns by doing. I would have benefited from more hands-on projects and fewer passive content modules. The flexibility helped with my schedule, but sometimes too much flexibility meant I'd fall behind.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made things easier. What made it harder was when things required constant internet access or real-time participation - my attendance suffered because of that. The peer support intervention actually helped a lot; having people to connect with made the course feel less isolating.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something, trying to understand the material but struggling to produce output. Or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. It could also be technical issues, or they're using the LMS to access materials they then work on elsewhere.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I passed and learned what I needed to. The learning happened offline, with peers, through practice. The platform is just one tool - it doesn't capture everything. Someone could look at my logs and think I wasn't trying, but I put in 26 hours a week.
Q22: Logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you click on, submission times. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the peer discussions, the real understanding. They see the surface but not the depth. They definitely can't measure whether someone actually learned anything.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, connecting it to things I know, trying to apply it. It's not about how many times I logged in - it's about whether my brain is working on the content. For me, engagement often looks like working through problems with my hands, discussing with peers, making mistakes and fixing them.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my peer study sessions, my offline practice, or the fact that I was scared but kept pushing through anyway. It wouldn't show that I'm 29, working, with limited resources, trying to better my situation. It wouldn't show my resilience or the support network that actually got me through this course.
